perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Participant shall hold in confidence the Confidential Information, not disclose the Confidential Information except to Receiving Participant ’s Representatives in acc ordance with this Agreement, and will exercise the same amount of diligence in preserving the secrecy of that information as the diligence used in preserving the secrecy of the Receiving Participant’s Confidential Information of like importance, but in no event less than reasonable diligence.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Participant shall endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence, and will attempt to exercise diligence in preserving the secrecy of that information, potentially similar to the diligence used in preserving the secrecy of the Receiving Participant’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a specific level of diligence. The modified text uses vague terms like 'endeavor,' 'attempt,' and 'potentially similar,' making the obligation unclear and unenforceable. The original specifies a baseline of 'reasonable diligence,' while the modified text removes that baseline.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines misappropriation, which includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, increasing the risk of misappropriation due to the vague language around the level of diligence required. The original text provides clear expectations, but the modified text fails to offer clear enforceable terms.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Participant's obligation to protect confidential information. The original text mandates a specific level of diligence (not less than reasonable diligence), while the changed text uses vague terms ('endeavor,' 'attempt,' and 'potentially similar'). This weakening of the obligation increases the risk of misappropriation, as defined in California Civil Code § 3426.1, which includes disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent. The scraped law snippet defines 'Misappropriation' as '(2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use'. By replacing the clear standard of 'reasonable diligence' with ambiguous language, the modified text undermines the enforceability of the duty to maintain secrecy, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Participants engaged in testing the interoperability of their respective products (“Test Objects”) will have joint and several ownership of the results of the interoperability testing insofar as they relate specifically to the interoperability of the Test Objects (the “Test Results”), information concerning which will constitute Confidential Information of the Participants.
perturbation.changed_text: The Participants engaged in testing the interoperability of their respective products (“Test Objects”) may share ownership of the results of the interoperability testing insofar as they relate to the interoperability of the Test Objects (the “Test Results”), information concerning which could be considered Confidential Information of the Participants.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that participants 'will have joint and several ownership.' The modified text replaces this with 'may share ownership,' making ownership discretionary. It also changes 'will constitute' to 'could be considered,' making the confidentiality of the results ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 201 addresses the ownership of copyright. The original text establishes joint ownership of test results, clarifying copyright ownership. The modified text introduces ambiguity by making ownership discretionary. This can create conflicts regarding the rights to reproduce, distribute, and display the copyrighted material (test reports), conflicting with the clear ownership guidelines established by copyright law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the initial obligation regarding ownership and confidentiality. The original text states participants 'will have joint and several ownership,' and information 'will constitute Confidential Information,' which implies a mandatory obligation. However, the modified text changes 'will have' to 'may share,' making ownership discretionary, and changes 'will constitute' to 'could be considered,' making confidentiality ambiguous. 17 U.S.C. § 201, as shown in both snippets, addresses copyright ownership and vests initial ownership in the author or authors of the joint work, implying a definitive assignment of rights. By making ownership discretionary, the modified contract introduces ambiguity that could conflict with the clear ownership guidelines established by copyright law.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Participant shall be liable for: (i) any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Participant, and (ii) any unauthorized discl osure or use of Confidential Information by persons or entities to whom the Receiving Participant has the right to disclose Confidential Information under this Agreement, EXCEPT THAT, TO THE FULL EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, THE RECEIVING PARTICIPANT SHALL N OT BE LIABLE FOR, AND HEREBY DISCLAIMS ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY DIRECT, INDIRECT, PUNITIVE, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF BUSINESS, REVENUE, PROFITS, GOODWILL, USE, DATA, OR OTHER ECONOMIC ADV ANTAGE) ARISING OUT OF, OR IN CONNECTION WITH INADVERTENT OR SUCH UNAUTHORIZED DISCLOSURE IF IT HAS EXERCISED THE SAME AMOUNT OF DILIGENCE IN PRESERVING THE SECRECY OF THAT INFORMATION AS THE DILIGENCE USED IN PRESERVING THE SECRECY OF THE RECEIVING PARTIC IPANT’S OWN CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OF LIKE IMPORTANCE, BUT IN NO EVENT LESS THAN REASONABLE DILIGENCE, AND, UPON BECOMING AWARE OF SUCH DISCLOSURE, NOTIFIED THE DISCLOSING PARTICIPANT THEREOF AND TAKEN REASONABLE MEASURES TO MITIGATE THE EFFECTS OF SUCH DISCLOSURE AND TO PREVENT ANY FURTHER DISCLOSURE.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Participant may be responsible for a breach of this Agreement, or unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information. The Receiving Participant might not be liable for damages arising from inadvertent disclosure if it has shown some diligence in preserving the secrecy of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly defines the liability of the receiving participant for breaches and unauthorized disclosures, providing specific conditions under which liability is limited. The modified text replaces 'shall be liable' with 'may be responsible' and 'might not be liable,' introducing uncertainty and weakening the enforcement of liability. The specificity of diligence required is removed, replaced by 'some diligence'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 347 outlines expectation damages as a standard remedy for breach of contract, aiming to put the injured party in as good a position as if the contract had been performed. By making liability discretionary and removing the clear expectation of diligence, the modified text undermines the principle of expectation damages, as it becomes unclear what level of compensation the disclosing party is entitled to in case of a breach. The original text provides a clearer basis for calculating damages by referencing the receiving party's diligence, whereas the modified version introduces ambiguity that can impede proper damage assessment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: restitution | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restitution Refers both to disgorging something which has been taken, and to compensation for loss or injury done. Wex commercial law criminal law wex definitions In civil cases: A remedy associated with [wex:unjust enrichment] in which the amount of recovery is typically based on the [wex:defendant]'s gain rather than the [wex:plaintiff]'s loss. In criminal cases: Full or partial compensation for loss paid by a criminal to a victim that is ordered as part of a criminal [wex:sentence] or as a condition of [wex:probation]. Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. (a) The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed but neither consequential or special damages nor penal damages may be had except as specifically provided in [the Uniform Commercial Code] or by other rule of law. (b) Any right or obligation declared by [the Uniform Commercial Code] is enforceable by action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited effect. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Participant's liability, replacing 'shall be liable' with 'may be responsible' and 'might not be liable.' This weakens the obligation and introduces uncertainty regarding the enforcement of liability, directly contradicting the principle that remedies should be liberally administered to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed...'. By creating ambiguity about the level of responsibility, it makes it harder to ascertain damages.

perturbation.original_text: The Open Mobile Alliance will exercise reasona ble efforts regarding its duties as set forth in Section 4 of this Agreement, provided, however that the Open Mobile Alliance shall not be liable for any breach of another Participant’s obligations hereunder. Participant agrees that the Open Mobile Allian ce will not be liable for any inadvertent disclosure by the Open Mobile Alliance of any information, including Confidential Information, under this Agreement. If any action is brought against the Open Mobile Alliance for any breach of its obligations here under, Participant agrees that the sole and exclusive remedy for any and all such actions brought by Participant against the Open Mobile Alliance shall not exceed in the aggregate ten thousand US dollars ($10,000 USD).
perturbation.changed_text: The Open Mobile Alliance will try to perform its duties as set forth in Section 4 of this Agreement, but the Open Mobile Alliance might not be liable for any breach of another Participant’s obligations. Participant agrees that the Open Mobile Alliance could have limited liability for any inadvertent disclosure. If any action is brought against the Open Mobile Alliance, Participant agrees that the remedy shall not automatically exceed ten thousand US dollars ($10,000 USD).
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses 'reasonable efforts' regarding its duties, and states the Open Mobile Alliance 'will not be liable.' The modified text replaces 'reasonable efforts' with 'try to perform,' 'shall not be liable' with 'might not be liable' and 'shall not exceed' with 'shall not automatically exceed', making the obligations and limitations of liability less definitive and creating ambiguity. It is unclear what 'try to perform' means, what the limited liability might look like, and what causes it to exceed the limit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304 implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. The modified text replaces 'reasonable efforts' with 'try to perform' regarding the Open Mobile Alliance's duties, suggesting a lower standard of performance. This could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith if 'trying' is not a sincere effort. The language on liability also becomes less definitive, making it harder to enforce good faith obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that undermine the obligation of good faith required by UCC § 1-304. Specifically, replacing 'reasonable efforts' with 'try to perform' suggests a lower standard of performance, potentially violating the good faith requirement. The original text's definitive language regarding liability is softened in the modified text, which further exacerbates the ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of obligations. This weakening and ambiguity are considered a significant contradiction under the definition provided.
